aliases: 广南黄姜米粉 (Guǎngnán Huángjiāng Mǐfěn)

chinese: 广南黄姜米粉 (Guǎngnán Huángjiāng Mǐfěn)

region_of_origin: Vietnam — Quang Nam province (Da Nang and surrounding area, Central Vietnam)
food_subgroup: Vietnamese turmeric rice noodle dish with minimal broth; wide flat turmeric-yellow rice noodles with mixed protein toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn URI exists for Mi Quang. No FoodOn term covers this Central Vietnamese turmeric noodle dish. Closest parent would be a generic noodle dish concept.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle dish — closest parent used for Vietnamese turmeric noodle dish (Mi Quang); no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this preparation
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0CMF is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a mixed noodle dish. No exact code exists for Mi Quang specifically.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for Mi Quang or Vietnamese turmeric noodle dishes.

preparation_method: assembled bowl with minimal broth dressing

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Mi Quang weighs approximately 400g, comprising wide flat turmeric rice noodles, pork ribs, shrimp, quail eggs, toasted peanuts, sesame rice crackers, fresh herbs, and approximately 50ml of broth used as a dressing rather than a full soup base.

nutrient_source.title: Mi Quang — FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table 2020 + USDA SR Legacy component calculation
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia + USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Mi Quang is not listed as a single entry in any approved primary source. Per-100g values were derived by component calculation using FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 for rice noodles, pork, shrimp, and quail egg components, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy for peanuts and sesame. The composite dish calculation reflects the standard bowl composition (~400g total: ~150g noodles, ~80g pork ribs, ~60g shrimp, ~30g quail eggs, ~20g peanuts, ~10g rice crackers, ~50ml broth, fresh herbs). Confidence: moderate (0.7).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of rice noodles — flat wide rice noodles analogous to kway teow / rice stick noodles
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mi Quang turmeric rice noodles. GI ~65 is estimated from the closest analogous food: wide flat rice noodles (kway teow / rice stick noodles), which are compositionally and texturally equivalent. University of Sydney GI Database lists flat rice noodles at GI ~61–65 (medium). GL per serving = (65 × 58g carbohydrate per bowl) / 100 = 37.7. The pork, shrimp, and peanut protein and fat content provide partial GI moderation. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 188mg/100g; ~750mg per bowl (37.5% of 2,000mg daily limit)
hypertension.guidance: Mi Quang contains approximately 750mg sodium per bowl — significantly lower than Pho Bo (~1,100mg) and Bun Bo Hue (~1,650mg) because only ~50ml of broth is used as a dressing rather than a full soup; hypertensive patients can consider Mi Quang as a relatively lower-sodium Vietnamese noodle option, though portion control and avoidance of added fish sauce at the table remain important.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~65 (medium), GL ~37.7 per bowl (high) — 58g carbohydrate per bowl from wide flat rice noodles and sesame rice crackers
type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load of ~37.7 per bowl is high for Type 2 Diabetes management; patients should request a reduced noodle portion (half serving), note that the 24g protein from pork and shrimp and the MUFA fat from toasted peanuts provide partial GI moderation, and monitor postprandial glucose response as the wide flat rice noodle format has a higher GI than thinner rice vermicelli.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.5g/100g (~6g per bowl); cholesterol ~180mg per bowl; sodium ~750mg per bowl
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mi Quang provides a moderate saturated fat load (~6g per bowl) from pork ribs and quail eggs, with cholesterol approximately 180mg per bowl; the toasted peanuts contribute beneficial MUFA, and the relatively lower sodium compared to other Vietnamese noodle soups makes it a preferable option for cardiovascular patients who wish to eat Vietnamese noodles, provided pork rib portions are moderated.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Turmeric in noodles (~20–30mg curcumin per bowl — clinically negligible); lower sodium than most Vietnamese noodle dishes
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The turmeric content in Mi Quang noodles provides approximately 20–30mg curcumin per bowl, which is clinically negligible as an anti-inflammatory dose; however, the lower sodium (~750mg) compared to other Vietnamese noodle dishes is beneficial for patients on corticosteroids who are managing fluid retention, and the peanuts and quail eggs contribute MUFA, selenium, and vitamin B12.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mi Quang (Mì Quảng) is a Central Vietnamese noodle dish originating from Quang Nam province, including the Da Nang area. It features wide, flat, turmeric-yellow rice noodles — the turmeric giving the noodles their distinctive golden colour — served with pork ribs, shrimp, quail eggs, toasted peanuts, sesame rice crackers (bánh tráng mè), and an assortment of fresh herbs including banana blossom (hoa chuối), lettuce, and mint. The defining characteristic that sets Mi Quang apart from all other Vietnamese noodle dishes is that it is served with only approximately 50ml of concentrated broth used as a flavour dressing, not as a full soup. This makes the bowl drier and more textured than Pho, Bun Bo Hue, or Bun Rieu. Per standard bowl (~400g): approximately 520 kcal, 24g protein, 18g fat, 58g carbohydrate, GI ~65 (medium), GL ~37.7 (high), and ~750mg sodium. The minimal broth format makes Mi Quang the lowest-sodium Vietnamese noodle dish — approximately 750mg sodium per bowl versus Pho Bo ~1,100mg and Bun Bo Hue ~1,650mg. Clinically, the GL of ~37.7 is high for Type 2 Diabetes management, but the sodium advantage makes it the preferred Vietnamese noodle choice for hypertensive patients. The toasted peanuts contribute MUFA fat and provide partial GI moderation. Nutrient values derived by component calculation using FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 and USDA SR Legacy. Confidence: moderate (0.7). Available at Vietnamese restaurants specialising in Central Vietnamese cuisine.

confidence_reason: Mi Quang is not listed as a single composite entry in any approved primary source (FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020, USDA SR Legacy, HPB Singapore). Per-100g values were derived by component calculation from approved sources for individual ingredients (rice noodles, pork, shrimp, quail egg, peanuts, sesame). GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (flat rice noodles) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium (0.7), not high.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT DERIVATION: Per-100g values are component-calculated estimates using FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 and USDA SR Legacy for individual ingredients. No single approved source entry exists for Mi Quang as a composite dish. 2. MONOUNSATURATED AND POLYUNSATURATED FAT: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0 — detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Vietnamese dish is not available as a single entry in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or USDA SR Legacy; component-level fatty acid data was not aggregated due to insufficient precision for the dish as prepared. 3. WATER: water_g is set to -1.0 — moisture content for this composite dish as served (with ~50ml broth dressing distributed across ~400g bowl) is not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA 2020 or USDA SR Legacy for this specific preparation. 4. TRANS FAT: trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero for this traditional preparation; no hydrogenated fats used. 5. GI ESTIMATION: GI ~65 is an analogous estimate based on wide flat rice noodles (kway teow / rice stick noodles) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Mi Quang turmeric noodles exists in the published literature. 6. SODIUM ADVANTAGE: The minimal broth format (~50ml vs ~300ml in full soup dishes) is the primary reason Mi Quang has the lowest sodium among Vietnamese noodle dishes (~750mg/bowl vs Pho ~1,100mg, Bun Bo Hue ~1,650mg). 7. HALAL STATUS: Mi Quang traditionally contains pork ribs; halal status is false. Shrimp-only versions may be available at halal establishments. 8. ONTOLOGY: FoodEx2 code A0CMF used as closest parent for noodle dish; no exact code exists for Mi Quang. FoodOn and SNOMED CT have no applicable codes for this dish.
